schools.summary: Chrysostom: the door is the Scriptures, the porter perhaps Moses; an allusion to false Christs; Theophylact makes the porter the Holy Spirit.
Augustine: the door is Christ himself — entering means preaching Christ in lowliness; the sheep are the foreknown/predestinated whom the Lord knows.
Gill/Calvin: Christ the only true shepherd; the Pharisees are thieves usurping a rule not given them.
A shepherd entering by the legitimate door; Lightfoot supplies the Talmudic sheepfold (gathering, milking, tithing).
Henry: Christ marks true from false shepherds, leaving the Pharisees to see themselves.

debates.question: What do the door and porter signify?

sides.position: door = the Scriptures; porter = Moses or the Spirit
door = Christ himself; porter = Christ or the Spirit

outliers.note: Solves why wolves sometimes hear and sheep stray by appeal to the foreknown/predestinated whom the Lord alone knows.

schools.summary: Here the shepherd = the gospel minister who enters by Christ the door; Ellicott links it to the sending of the Seventy toward the 'other sheep.'
Clarke: the marks of a good pastor — a lawful entrance by the internal call of Christ, aiming at God's glory and the salvation of souls.
Calvin: God, not the world's applause, approves the true shepherd who enters rightly.
The porter is the doorkeeper who opens the fold by morning; the sheep know the shepherd's voice (Robertson doubts every detail is allegorical).
Wesley: the doorkeeper may be styled the Father, who opens the door of faith (Acts 14:27).
Augustine: 'by name' = the names of the predestinated; he leads them out by loosing the chain of their sins.
He puts forth 'all' his own, overlooking none, and goes before to find pasture and ward off danger — the tender care of the shepherd of souls.
Clarke/Wesley: the pastor goes before by example as well as precept; the sheep follow with the witness in themselves of his words.
Calvin: the sheep follow because the shepherd's voice is God's, not man's.
The 'stranger' is any voice not the shepherd's; the sheep by no means follow, discerning the false from the true (Isa 1:3).
Clarke/Wesley: the sheep detect and flee a stranger to salvation 'as from the plague,' finding nothing of God in his voice.
Gill: the stranger is one not sent by Christ and not preaching him.
The word is 'paroimia' (a wayside saying/allegory), John's term, not the Synoptic 'parabolē'; they grasped neither its meaning nor design.
Augustine: the Lord feeds by plain words and exercises by obscure ones, the obscurity sifting the humble from the scornful.
Calvin: their proud self-conceit made them dull in a matter not even very obscure.
Henry: they did not understand because they would not; the Spirit makes Christ known.
Not a new allegory but the unfolding of door and shepherd; Christ is the legitimate door of access to the fold (Eph 2:18).
Chrysostom: the door he had shut he now opens — he is himself the door; Lightfoot: Christ's flock has a new mark, not mere Abrahamic descent.
Calvin: all spiritual doctrine that feeds souls consists in Christ alone (1 Cor 2:2).
Calvin/Gill: not the true prophets, but all false doctrine and false prophets contrasted with the Gospel; the OT denounces such shepherds (Jer 23:1; Ezek 34:2).
Clarke: 'came instead of me' = false Christs like Theudas/Judas (Acts 5:36-37); Wesley: the Pharisees' self-appointed rule over consciences.
Textual note on 'before me'; the reference is to false Messiahs and self-appointed leaders, not the prophets.

debates.question: Whom does 'all that came before me' condemn?

schools.summary: The emphatic 'by me' — 'the narrowness of truth'; parallel to the strait gate (Mt 7:13-14); 'go in and out' = the whole of a safe, supplied life.
Calvin: the believer's highest consolation — once in Christ, out of danger, kept safe and fed to the full.
Clarke/Wesley: a threefold gift — salvation, liberty, and pasture — entered through faith and kept from the wolf.
The thief's escalating ruin (steal, kill, destroy) against Christ's gift of life 'more abundantly' — a superadded fulness.
Clarke: mercenary clergy who enter for revenue are the basest thieves with deadly doctrine; Wesley: such is any who enters not by Christ.
Calvin: a warning against drowsy indifference to Satan's ministers; Henry: Christ knows and keeps his chosen.
'The shepherd the good (kalos) one' — the beautiful, ideal shepherd who exposes his life rather than flee the wolf.
Augustine: Christ is both Door and Shepherd; the good shepherd is known by giving his life, marking him off from the hireling.
Calvin: his extraordinary love for the sheep does not spare his own life — to reject such a shepherd is gross ingratitude.
The hireling is a low-status hired labourer with no interest in the sheep — the unfaithful minister who flees danger.
Wesley: not receiving hire but loving the hire above the work makes a hireling; Clarke: every party may have one, the danger greatest where pay is largest.
Gill/Calvin: faithful ministers worthy of reward are not hirelings; even imperfect teachers of pure doctrine are to be heard.

themes: loving the hire above the flock

schools.summary: He has no shepherd-heart — 'it is no care to him about the sheep'; the opening clause is likely a textual addition.
Calvin: the day of contest exposes the hireling's treachery — he serves the wages, not the flock that is not his own.
Wesley: he flees because he loves the hire, not the sheep.
'Know' = affectionate, intimate regard (explained by 'loveth' in v.17); the sheep partake of his nature — the secret of their love.
Chrysostom: to preside over a church needs the courage to lay down life for the sheep — the weight of the shepherd's office.
Calvin: the knowing proceeds from love and care, while he disregards all who will not obey the Gospel.
The sheep's knowledge of the shepherd is likened to the Father–Son knowledge; Christ knows the Father and so reveals him (John 1:18; 17:21).
Calvin: avoid thorny speculation — Christ is the bond between God and us, unable to forget us as surely as the Father cannot reject him.
Wesley: 'I lay down my life' in the present — his whole life was a going unto death.
The 'other sheep' are the Gentiles (and Samaritans); Christ's horizon takes in all races (John 11:52; 12:32; Acts 18:10).
Calvin: undoubtedly the calling of the Gentiles, joined to the ancient 'fold' of God's heritage.
Wesley: 'one flock' not 'one fold' (a misprint) — one undivided flock under one shepherd; he dies for every man (Heb 2:9).
The Father's love is drawn out by the Son's voluntary self-offering (John 3:16; Phil 2:9); the laying down aims at the taking again.
Calvin: a higher eternal love grounds the Father's love, yet the Son's dying makes our salvation dearer to him than his own life.
Clarke/Wesley: not weakness but free purpose — he dies to expiate sin, to rise again for our justification.
The wholly voluntary death — none could take it by force (John 19:11); he lays it down and takes it again by his own power.
Calvin: a consolation — he dies not by constraint but offers himself willingly, free from the necessity that binds us.
Origen/Cyril: he came to his passion willingly, foretelling it beforehand.
'Schisma' again (cf. 7:43; 9:16) — division over Jesus is a recurring mark in John; the Pharisees lead the hostile party.
Henry: the contrast of his gracious words and their spiteful ones threw them into heats and parties.
Calvin: the doctrine wins some disciples and many adversaries, splitting what had seemed one body.
The recurring slander 'he hath a demon and is mad' (cf. 7:20; 8:48; Mark 3:21) — an easy way to discount Jesus.
Calvin: the most offensive reproach, devised to make men shudder at hearing him and silence every word.

themes: he hath a devil and is mad

schools.summary: The better party answers: his words are calm and sober, and 'can a demon open the eyes of the blind?' (cf. John 9:6) — an unanswerable question.
Calvin: an appeal to the fact itself — truth maintains itself, and no malice can keep God's power from shining in the Gospel.
Cyril joins the opened eyes to the Messianic sign (Isa 35:5).
The Maccabean feast of Dedication (Hanukkah), instituted 164 B.C. after Antiochus' defilement, kept eight days from 25 Chislev; ~two months after Tabernacles.
Calvin: 'enkainia' = renewal, the temple reconsecrated by Judas Maccabaeus, kept yearly to recall God's grace.
Wesley: Christ observed a festival of human appointment (1 Macc 4:59) — warrant that such observance may be innocent for us.
The 'picturesque imperfect' — Jesus walking in Solomon's eastern colonnade (Acts 3:11; 5:12), a relic surviving till Titus; eyewitness memory.
Calvin/Gill: he walked to keep warm in winter; the 'temple' is the later structure bearing the old name.
They encircle him ('ekyklōsan'); 'How long dost thou hold our souls in suspense?' — eyewitness scene; Lightfoot: they feared rather than hoped he was Messiah.
Calvin: a cunning attack to draw the word from him before the crowd, that a mob or the Romans might destroy him.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns Herod and does not bear on v.24.

schools.summary: An indirect answer — the word 'Messiah' was impossible given their political misconception; his works in the Father's name already bear witness (John 5:36).
Calvin: he does not conceal that he is the Christ but reproaches their obstinate malice — the fault of not knowing him is their own.
Calvin: they believe not because they are reprobate — faith is a special gift; their unbelief detracts nothing from the Gospel.
Clarke/Wesley: not a divinely-imposed inability — they are 'not sheep' because they proudly refuse to hear and follow.
Barnes names the moral cause — pride, prejudice, vainglory keep them from the evidence (John 8:47).

debates.question: Does unbelief follow from not being elect sheep, or are they 'not sheep' because they refuse to follow?

sides.position: they believe not because they are not of the elect
they are not sheep because they will not hear and follow

schools.summary: The threefold mark — hear, are known, follow — repeats vv.4, 14, in contrast with the doubting Jews.
Calvin: an argument from contraries — God effectually calls the elect, so the sheep are proved by their faith and obedience.
Wesley: three conditions (hear by faith, are approved as loving, follow) with three promises annexed.
Eternal life given now; emphatic double negative 'shall never perish'; none shall snatch them from his hand (John 6:40; 17:12).
Calvin: an inestimable fruit of faith — assurance of security, resting on Christ the faithful guardian and the power of God.
Clarke: security bound to the marks of v.27 — those who continue to hear and follow shall never perish.
A textual crux ('who is greater' vs neuter 'that which he gave is greater'); either way the Father's supreme power over all secures the sheep (John 6:37).
Clarke: the Father is mightier than all the energies of men and demons — the lover of God need fear nothing.

debates.question: Is the text 'the Father who is greater than all' or 'that which he gave me is greater than all'?

sides.position: the neuter reading (the gift is greater)
the Father is greater than all

schools.summary: Neuter 'hen' = one essence/nature, not one person; 'we are' refutes Sabellius, 'one' refutes Arius (Robertson, Wesley).
Wesley/Clarke: unity not of will only but of power and nature — a claim no mere man could make without blasphemy.
Calvin: in context it asserts the agreement and shared power that secures the sheep, though grounded in unity of nature.

debates.question: Does 'I and the Father are one' argue first from essence or from shared operation/power?

sides.position: in context unity of operation/power securing the sheep, grounded in nature

themes: I and the Father are one

schools.summary: Stoning was the blasphemer's penalty (Lev 24:14-16); 'again' recalls 8:59 — they grasped his claim to equality with God.
Augustine: 'I and my Father are one' kindled their rage; Hilary turns it against heretics who deny what the Jews understood.
Calvin: unbelief is the mother of rage — their intent in questioning (v.24) is now exposed in their thirst for slaughter.
'Many good (kala, noble) works from the Father' — beneficent healings; he appeals to them against the stones.
Calvin: a charge of ingratitude — to requite God's manifold favours with cruelty; the works are God's, so their rage is against God.
Clarke: a catalogue of mercies — healing, delivering, feeding, teaching freely — 'and is this my reward?'
Their own indictment ('for blasphemy... makest thyself God') is a hostile witness that they rightly heard v.30 as a claim to deity (John 5:18).
Calvin: they cloak their rage in zeal for God's glory — wicked men never sin without a plausible pretext.
From Psalm 82:6 ('law' = whole OT); an a fortiori argument (Hillel's first canon) — 'gods' = the judges who bore God's word.
Calvin: he clears himself not by denying he is God's Son but by maintaining he justly said so, exposing their malice.
The judges were 'gods' as bearers of God's commission; 'the scripture cannot be broken' = its authority is final and unbreakable (Mt 5:19).
Calvin: magistracy is appointed by God's will (Rom 13:1-2); the argument rests on the unbreakable authority of Scripture.
Wesley: 'cannot be broken' = nothing written therein can be censured or rejected.
'Sanctified' = set apart/consecrated to the Messianic office and Incarnation; every word contrasts him with the mere judges of Ps 82:6.
Calvin: a far higher consecration than believers' — he alone separated that the Spirit's power and God's majesty be displayed (John 6:27).
Wesley: a sanctification prior to his mission implying Christ is God in the highest sense, infinitely above those judges.
He rests his case on doing 'the works of my Father' (which God only can do), repeating his claim to sonship and deity (John 5:17, 36).
Calvin: a gracious concession — believe only on the plain evidence of the miracles, an evident proof of his Divinity.
Clarke: 'if I do not do such works as God only can perform, then believe me not.'
'Believe the works' — irrefutable — leading to knowledge of the mutual indwelling 'the Father in me, and I in him' (John 5:36; 17:21).
Calvin: they are convicted of sacrilegious contempt for denying reverence to what are undeniably the works of God.
Wesley: knowledge may precede or follow faith; 'the Father in me' and 'I and the Father are one' illustrate each other.

themes: Father in me, I in him

schools.summary: They 'kept on seeking' to seize him as before (7:30, 44; 8:20); he escaped, having left them with the impression of a claim to deity.
Bede/Augustine: they persist in madness, seeking him with bloodthirsty violence; Theophylact: he withdraws to further the faith of the willing.
Calvin: his escape was a wonderful exertion of divine power restraining their lawless passions; Henry: their hearts were hardened.
He returns to Bethany/Bethabara beyond Jordan (John 1:28), the site of John's early baptizing, and abode there.
Calvin/Gill: a strategic withdrawal — not for ease but to preach and erect a sanctuary in the wilderness after Jerusalem drove him out.
Wesley: the place where John baptized and gave so honourable a testimony of him.
John wrought no signs but his witness proves true now the people see Jesus; 'prepared soil for Christ' — the light never absent (John 1:5).
Calvin: the large assembly shows he withdrew not to cease his duty but to build a sanctuary in the wilderness after Jerusalem's rejection.
Wesley: 'John did no miracle' — an honour reserved for the One whose forerunner he was.
'There many believed' (emphatic) — they recognized in Jesus the original John had drawn (John 1:29-34); reception contrasts Jerusalem's rejection.
Gill: this is why Christ left Jerusalem — there were others appointed to believe (John 11:52).
Clarke: they believed on a double ground — the Baptist's trusted testimony and the miracles confirming it.
